data.title: Dividend Champion, Contender, And Challenger Highlights: Week Of October 20
data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with over 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: HP Remains An Interesting Technology Utility
data.description:
If you use a printer or a PC, there is a 1 out of 5 chances that it will be an HP (NYSE:HPQ). The company has become almost synonymous with printers and personal computers – both in the workplace and at homes. HP has been missing from the hype and headlines of AI-related excitement, which has been preserved lately for semiconductor companies, followed by social media and other household technology stocks. But HP is right in the middle of the semiconductor and AI software chain; semiconductors for consumers are fitted in PCs or smartphones, and AI-software has to run on hardware; again smartphones or PCs. HP is not only in the midst of it all, its products are an essential component of the business and consumer technological developments.

PC and printer demand will remain stable – with slow growth

Despite the doom and gloom for the PC market following the impressive rise of smartphones and tablets in the past 15 years or so, the PC market has proved how essential are, and will remain. Almost 15 years after the first iPad was introduced, it is clear now that no office environment can use tablets to replace PCs.

And this reflects clearly in industry numbers; PC shipments have been relatively stable over the past 10-15 years, with some ebbs and flows, but with no major secular downward trend. PC global shipments are still double those of tablets – although tablets are growing four times faster. But HP should not be too concerned about that; HP is not claiming to be competing head-to-head with tablets, it is focusing on a completely different niche of work and gaming equipment that is a different realm from that of tablets. HP generates mere USD 35 billion of annual personal systems sales in a personal systems market that is forecasted to reach USD 537 billion in 2026. The market will remain, and HP will continue to have a leading position in it.

With regards to printing; despite all the environmental and sustainability pressures to cut printing needs, the printing market is still alive and kicking. Despite a steady decline in the past 15 years, the market has largely stabilised and is currently estimated by HP to reach a value of USD 188 billion in 2026, of which HP has a 25% market share. The fast-growing development of 3D printing provides a hedge versus the decline in traditional printing; 3D printing was in its infancy only a few years ago, now it is making solid leaps with growth of 20-25% annually.

Can HP continue to be safe ‘technology utility’ for investors?

HP’s management continue to be conservative and realistic; focusing on expanding their core business along with developing technologies and trends, while avoiding risky adventures. This strategy should ensure that HP remains ‘safe’, while continuing to expand its business slowly but surely, and expanding along with it free cash flow generation and shareholder returns.

HP has identified five core growth areas that it expects to drive revenue expansion: video conferencing systems, gaming computers, workforce systems, printing consumer subscriptions, industrial graphics and 3D printing. These are rational, progressive areas, that are within HP’s core competencies, that are in demand, and that should support future growth. HP reported that several of those areas reported double-digit growth in Q3 2024 versus the previous year. The company expected these areas to add USD 5 billion to sales by 2026 – from 2022, achieving 13% CAGR. While these are not the stratospheric growth levels of Nvidia and the likes; they ensure steady growth and less volatility for the business and for the share price.

Solid financial performance supports growth in shareholder returns

In the third financial quarter of 2024, ending 28th of August 2024, the company reported total revenues of USD 13.5 billion – an anaemic 2% growth from the previous year. 69% of revenues came from Personal Systems - which are mainly notebooks and desktops – similar levels to the past two years. Printing, however, is a lot more profitable; generating 54% of total operating profits, due to an operating margin of 17.3% versus a low margin of 6.4% for personal systems. This is due to higher cost components of producing PCs, as well as lower bargaining power of HP and PC producers, as well as more intense competition in the PC industry. Operating profit margin of 9.6% is far from impressive, but free cash flow generation of USD 1.3 billion – equal exactly to the operating cash flow generated – is definitely impressive, attesting to the company’s efficiency in managing its cash flows and in returning most of it (USD 0.9 billion) to shareholders in share buybacks and dividends. The Q4 and full financial year results should be due soon to give a full picture of how well HP is progressing.

Valuation is still reasonable, despite share price near record highs

HP’s share price increased by almost 40% over the past year – in line with NASDAQ and the S&P 500, and ahead of tech giants such as Alphabet and Microsoft. True, HP was probably too undervalued a year ago, but at P/E of 13 times, it is still far from expensive. When I wrote about HP in February 2023, HP’s share price had dropped by 30% over the previous year, and was trading at a very low valuation of 9.8 times P/E and 6.5 times market cap to operating cash flow. At today’s P/E of 13 times, HP is still trading lower than peers Dell which is trading at 26 times P/E, although Lenovo which is trading at 16 times P/E.

Aggressive shareholder returns strengthen the valuation metrics. USD 29 billion have been paid since 2016 on share buybacks and dividends - a whopping 80% of the current market cap. This is one of the main reasons Berkshire Hathaway would have acquired its stake, as Warren Buffett has always been a strong supporter of share buybacks to boost investor returns. The company has managed to keep its free cash flow generation at elevated levels – around USD 3-4 billion in each of the past few years. The company expects free cash flow to decline to USD 3.1-3.6 billion in the 2024 financial year.

HP can maintain its investment pitch as a utility technology company, with relative stable and consistent returns to shareholders. To maintain this status, the company has to continue keeping apace of the fast-changing computing market, and to keep the printing business stable and highly profitable. Keeping cost measures tight is also essential. If it manages to do that, HP will remain an interesting technology utility investment for more years to come – but at a right price.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - PMI Data And IMF Meeting Dominate The Agenda
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in Review: US Earnings Surprise and Gold Hits Fresh Highs

The week drew to a close with fresh highs for the S&P 500 and Gold, while US earnings continued on its impressive path. As markets digest US earnings, rate cut bets in the US remain steady from a week ago. Markets are still pricing in around a 92.3% chance of a 25 bps cut from the Fed in November, up from 89.5% last week.

On the whole, the week itself was a bit of a hit-and-miss, with quite a bit of choppy price action in the early part of the week. However, UK data and the ECB interest rate meeting have seen markets face up to the fact that the global rate environment is set for a correction. The Bank of England (BOE), which was expected to see its policy diverge from the Fed and ECB, has found itself in the spotlight as softer inflation figures ramped up rate cut expectations.

The impact of this realization saw the US Dollar emerge as the front-runner for the week once more. The greenback continued its impressive run of late as we approached the US elections in November.

Looking at fellow commodities and metals, oil prices continued its slide this week. Softer data globally and renewed concerns around Chinese growth saw both OPEC + and the IEA downgrade their forecasts once more. Silver breached the 32.00 handle and, similar to gold, analysts are predicting a further 12 months of bullish price action with a target price for silver set at $45/oz.

On the FX front, as mentioned, the US Dollar continued its rise this week. The Japanese Yen experienced whiplash price action as news filtered through of a possible rate hike, which was immediately rebuked by the BoJ.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has enjoyed a renaissance this week with the world's largest cryptocurrency having risen to trade at 68500 at the time of writing. This leaves the world's largest cryptocurrency just 8% of its all-time highs.

The Week Ahead: US Earnings, IMF Meeting, Central Banks and PMI

The week ahead is a busy one, underlined by the IMF meeting taking place in Washington next week. This is but one pillar of what is shaping up to be a busy week for markets across the globe.

We have PMI data from a host of countries, a couple of Central Bank meetings, US earnings and geopolitical developments to consider. One has to wonder which event may deliver the greatest impact where markets are concerned and that is a tough question to answer.

Asia Pacific Markets

In Asia, the week is quite, following a busy period that culminated with a data dump from China this week. The biggest event in Asia will likely be Tokyo inflation data. Given the mixed rhetoric around rate hikes by the Bank of Japan (BoJ), the inflation data could meaningfully influence the timing of the Bank of Japan's next move.

Australia and New Zealand will also be enjoying a quite week, with both countries likely to be affected by external factors in the week ahead. There is a speech by RBNZ Governor Adrian Orr which could affect the NZD.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the European Central Bank has seen an increase in the probability of a 50 bps cut in December, as much as they have tried to avoid it. Growth in the Euro area has become a key area of focus and that makes next week a big one as PMIs will be crucial for the eurozone.

Since May, the composite PMI has generally been declining, except for a temporary boost in August due to the Olympics. September's reading fell below 50, indicating contraction and heightening fears of a potential recession. While these fears might be exaggerated, an economic slowdown seems likely. The October figures will reveal if there's any improvement; if not, concerns about a slowdown will intensify. Poor PMIs could send the Euro spiraling lower, as market participants are likely to increase bets on a 50 bps rate cut.

The UK has just come off a week that has shifted the narrative for the Bank of England. A significant slowdown in inflation, more importantly services inflation, and rate cuts are back on. Service PMIs have been gradually declining, aligning with slower growth rates in the year's second half. If this trend persists, coupled with the recent slowdown in services inflation, the BoE might accelerate its rate cuts. Keep an eye out for any indications of this during Governor Bailey's appearances in Washington next week.

The US data calendar is pretty bare next week, with the Fed's beige book being the biggest event. I expect we will hear more chatter from Federal Reserve Policymakers, which could add volatility to markets. It will be interesting to gauge if the US Dollar can hold on to recent gains.

Canada will enjoy a bigger week, with the Bank of Canada (BOC) rate decision expected on Wednesday. The market expects a big change due to low inflation and weaker activity, but I think the decision will be tight, with most officials cautious about moving too quickly. Markets are pricing in a 91.4% chance that the BoC will deliver a 50 bps cut.

Geopolitical developments and US earnings could have a major impact next week. I would also say that any comments from Central Bank Governors at the IMF may be worth paying attention to as policy rates remain a hot topic globally.

Chart of the Week

This week's focus is back on the US Dollar Index (DXY) as we have a lack of data, which could mean the technicals become more important.

Having enjoyed a stellar rally, the DXY appears to have finally found resistance at the 200-day MA. There are signs that point to a retracement, but given the overarching macroeconomic outlook, the downside might be limited. The Fed is now expected to cut less than the BoE and the ECB, which is contributing to the USD rise over the month of October. The RSI on the daily has now flashed a potential sell signal, having broken back below the 70 level from overbought territory. This is usually a sign of shifting momentum and that a drop in price may continue.

The DXY is also caught between the 100- and 200-day MA, with the 100-day MA likely to serve as a key support area around 103.180. A break below this support level could open up a retest of 102.65 and potentially 102.100.

Now a break above the 200-day MA remains possible and could open up a run toward the 104.00 and 104.50 handle.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart - October 18, 2024

Key Levels to Consider:

Support:

  • 103.18
  • 102.65
  • 102.00

Resistance:

  • 104.00
  • 104.50
  • 105.00
  • 105.63

Original Post

data.title: Steady Cash Flow Meets Tech Titans: A Look At YMAG
data.description:
Investment Summary

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) is a member of the YieldMax series, which allocates funds to their ETFs on the "Magnificent 7 Stocks". This refers to seven of the world's largest and most influential technology companies (AAPL, AMZN, GOOG, META, MSFT, NVDA, and TSLA). For each of these stocks, YieldMax has a dedicated ETF that actively trades options on the underlying stock:

YMAG employs a fund-of-funds strategy, holding equal-weight positions in seven YieldMax ETFs and rebalances its holdings monthly to ensure equal exposure to each constituent, creating a diversified income stream. This income is then distributed to the investors monthly. The current distribution rate is 63% (on an annualized basis). For this service, the fund charges 1% in gross expenses.

In this article, we will have a look at how the YMAG fund has performed, and we will compare this performance with that of the underlying ETFs.

Fund Characteristics

YMAG rotates its holdings in their 7 ETFs tracking the Magnificent 7 stocks and receives monthly income from each holding. This income is then distributed to the owners of YMAG shares. The income from the underlying ETFs is derived from trading options on the underlying stocks and investing the excess cash in short-term US Treasuries.

  • Fund Name: YMAG
  • ETF Exposure: APLY, MSFO, FBY, AMZY, GOOY, TSLY, NVDY
  • Stock Exposure: Apple Inc., Microsoft Corporation, Amazon.com Inc, Alphabet Inc, Tesla Inc and Meta Platforms Inc.
  • Net Assets: $171.7 million
  • Shares Outstanding: 9,200,000
  • Inception Date: January 30, 2024
  • Gross Expense Ratio: 1.28%

The fund is quite small, it only holds $172 million in assets, and it's been around for 10 months since its inception date. The 1.28% gross expense ratio is relatively high compared with the expense ratio of the underlying ETFs (which is 0.99%). The underlying funds are all concentrated on the main technology stocks and represent a significant portion of the S&P 500 (SPY). The portfolio reallocates its monthly positions to ensure the weighting remains equal among its components.

Investment Strategy

The investment strategy of YMAG is relatively straightforward (invest 1/7th in each of its components), but the strategy of the underlying ETFs is more complex. These ETFs trade options on the Magnificent Seven stocks in order to generate income and distribute it to the investors.

The option strategies YieldMax is applying mainly fall under 2 categories:

1) Standard Strategy: Selling Synthetic Covered Calls on the underlying stock. This means the fund will sell put options on the underlying stock and simultaneously buy call options to simulate a synthetic long position in the stock. Once this is done, a call option is written on this position to generate option income by collecting the premium of the sold call option.

2) Opportunistic Strategy: Selling Credit Call spreads. To carry out this strategy, the fund will sell an out-of-the-money call option and buy a call option which is even further out of the money. For FBY for example, the fund currently has the following positions:

  • Short META $595 call option expiring on October 25th, 2024
  • Long META $610 call option expiring on October 25th, 2024

This option spread position will have a P&L profile which is determined by the price action of META stock, which is currently at $580. If the stock price remains at this level until October 25th, the call spread will have obtained the full credit spread between both options.

Income-focused option strategies like covered calls and short call/put spreads trade higher potential profits for a guaranteed income stream. This means that while investors can collect premiums, they also limit their upside exposure.

Each of the underlying ETFs gains income via these options trades and then distributes it to their investors. YMAG is investing approximately $172 million in these 7 stocks and receives income from all 7 ETFs. This is then distributed to the investors of YMAG. The below table shows the historical distributions investors of YMAG would have received.

The last distributed amount was $0.9231 which is comparable to a dividend being paid out on a stock with a dividend yield of approximately 63%.

Historical Performance

The Magnificent Seven stocks have historically been volatile, high-growth companies. This volatility creates favorable conditions for options trading, and YMAG benefits from the inflated premiums generated by selling options on these stocks.

While YMAG’s strategy limits its potential upside by capping gains through options trading, it compensates by providing reliable monthly income. This trade-off is attractive for income-focused investors who are willing to forego some capital appreciation in exchange for regular payouts.

When we compare the performance of YMAG with the underlying performance of the Magnificent Seven stocks (we'll use Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF here, MAGS) and the Nasdaq (QQQ) we can see YMAG delivered 24% since the end of January 2024, underperforming the Magnificent Seven's ROI of 38%, but outperforming the Nasdaq which delivered 18% to its investors.

Since YMAG is a new ETF, performance data is limited, but given the track records of the underlying YieldMax ETFs, the fund is expected to provide substantial monthly income to its investors. The reallocation strategy, which ensures equal exposure to each of the Magnificent 7, helps mitigate the risk of underperformance in any one stock while providing steady cash flow from options premiums.

Implied volatility

One key driver of the income of the option strategies is the implied volatility of the underlying stocks. This implied volatility is one of the key components that drives the premium price of the options the YieldMax funds are trading. Generally, the higher the implied volatility, the higher the earned premiums.

The elevated implied volatility of several Magnificent Seven stocks has made options trading in them a lucrative venture.

In the chart above we can see the historical IV levels of TSLA and NVDA, which have been fluctuating around the 40%-60% levels. When implied volatility reaches these levels, option prices typically become expensive. This phenomenon explains how the YMAG holdings can offer such substantial distribution rates to their investors.

The high implied volatility of YMAG's underlying holdings is a double-edged sword. While it increases the value of the options, which benefits shareholders, it also suggests the potential for significant price swings, which could lead to losses. As explained in the section here above, the holdings of YMAG offer no protection against this downside risk.

Risks & Considerations

Investing in YMAG comes with some risks, just like any other option-income ETF. If the Magnificent 7 stocks take a big dive, even YMAG's clever options strategies won't completely shield you from losses. While the options income can help cushion the blow, your overall investment will still feel the impact.

Another thing to watch out for is volatility. The options premiums that YMAG's ETFs collect are closely tied to how much the Magnificent 7 stocks are expected to fluctuate. If things calm down, the income from the ETF might also shrink.

Finally, the fund’s expense ratio, like other YieldMax ETFs, may be higher than traditional index ETFs due to the active management of the options strategies.

Conclusion

YMAG is a great option for investors who want to invest in the biggest tech companies around and earn extra cash each month. This cash income does come at a cost, as the upside potential of the tech stocks is capped.

YMAG acts like a basket of different YieldMax ETFs that focus on the Magnificent 7, spreading your risk and making it a good fit for those seeking income from the biggest tech stocks.

Investors should be mindful of the downside risks of options strategies, such as declining volatility and the potential for substantial losses, especially if the underlying stocks experience a sharp decline.

data.title: Q3 Earnings Season Kicks Off With Better-Than-Expected Bank Results
data.description:
The earnings season started off strong with some positive results from the big banks, which all blew away analyst estimates. Results mostly impressed investors who responded positively to reports on Friday and Tuesday morning, with the latter eventually overshadowed by a Nvidia (NVDA) sell-off which took markets lower that day. Morgan Stanley (MS) got things back on track on Wednesday with impressive results which led the major indices higher.

However, it was earnings results from another sector's constituent on Thursday that drove markets. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (TSM) saw their stock soar after posting robust profits due to increased AI demand, which the company said should continue for "many years."1 As a result, other big AI players such as Nvidia and AMD (AMD) and, to a lesser degree, Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT), saw their stocks move higher, just ahead of the big tech earnings season which kicks off the week of October 28.

One major thesis leading into this earnings season was that lowered sell-side estimates heading into reports would lead to bigger beats and happier investors. That's more or less what happened with the banks, with some caveats, of course.

Are Banks Getting their Flowers?

All six of the big banks beat analysts' profit expectations, and all but Wells Fargo (WFC) surpassed revenue estimates.2 Most of these names saw their stocks rewarded as a result of better-than-expected results, even WFC's stock price popped 6% on the day of their earnings report despite missing on the top-line. On the flip-side, Citigroup (C) beat both metrics but still saw their stock slide 5% (after initially popping up 1%) as a result of increasing loan loss provisions.3 Bank of America (BAC) also reported positive surprises, but the stock ended the day flat.4

Overall, the takeaways from the big banks were bullish.

On the consumer: The story on the health of US consumers remains the same, while spending activity remains intact, consumers remain cautious and credit cards are getting paid off at a slower rate than usual.5 Lower rates have encouraged more borrowing, but real estate hasn't quite seen the relief expected.

Investment banking: The investment banks (specifically, Goldman Sachs6 and Morgan Stanley7) performed well in Q3 due to an increase in fees and increased activity specifically in M&A. With the Federal Reserve implementing a jumbo rate cut last month and expected to continue cutting interest rates at the next several meetings, that should make borrowing money more enticing and therefore increase the deal pipeline.

Trading: Equities trading revenues boosted all of the big bank's bottom lines, while FICC trading came in lower, and in some cases, negative on a YoY basis.

Overall: The soft landing is here according to JPMorgan, with CFO Jeremy Barnum commenting "these results are consistent with a soft landing" on their conference call8, and similar sentiment was indicated by their peers as well.

Q3 Earnings Scorecard

For the Q3 season, S&P 500 EPS growth is expected to hit 4.1%, which would be the fifth consecutive quarter of bottom-line growth.9 Note that 4.1% is a blended growth rate that includes results for those companies that reported as of Friday, October 11 and estimates for those that have yet to report. Revenues are expected to come in even higher with 4.6% YoY growth.10

On a sector level, Information Technology, Communication Services and Health Care all remain leaders on the top and bottom line, while Energy is the only sector expected to post YoY declines on both metrics.11

More Companies Pushing Back Q3 Earnings Dates - Likely Due to Upcoming US Presidential Election

After falling to its lowest level in its nine years in the first quarter of 2024, the Late Earnings Report Index, our proprietary measure of CEO uncertainty, has been higher for the last two quarters.

The LERI tracks outlier earnings date changes among publicly traded companies with market capitalizations of $250M and higher. The LERI has a baseline reading of 100, and anything above that indicates that companies are feeling uncertain about their current and short-term prospects. A LERI reading under 100 suggests that companies feel they have a pretty good crystal ball for the near term.

The official pre-peak season LERI reading for Q3 (data collected in Q4) stands at 278, well above the baseline reading. Typically, this would suggest that companies are feeling less certain about economic conditions, but this quarter we believe there is another culprit. On November 5, the US will elect its next president, and that week is typically a peak one for the Q3 season. Many companies that would usually report that week have pushed off their announcement to the following two weeks to avoid getting lost in the election coverage. As of October 11, there were 105 late outliers and 34 early outliers.

On Deck Next Week

Next week is not quite peak season yet, but contains some highly watched earnings reports. Results from the Magnificent 7 will start to trickle in when Tesla reports on Wednesday. We'll also get a read on the Industrials sector next week when 3M, Texas Instruments and Honeywell report. Expectations for Industrials remain low, with profits only expected to grow 1% YoY and revenue growth estimates at 0.1%.12

Q3 Earnings Wave

This earnings season, the peak weeks will fall between October 28 and November 15, with each week expected to see over 2,000 reports. Currently, November 7 is predicted to be the most active day with 1,475 companies anticipated to report. Thus far, only 59% of companies have confirmed their earnings date (out of our universe of 11,000+ global names), so this is subject to change. The remaining dates are estimated based on historical reporting data.

Footnotes

  1. TSMC Reports Third Quarter EPS of NT$12.54, October 17, 2024.
  2. Wells Fargo Reports Third Quarter 2024 Net Income of $5.1 billion, or $1.42 per Diluted Share, October 11, 2024.
  3. THIRD QUARTER 2024 RESULTS AND KEY METRICS, Citigroup, October 15, 2024.
  4. Bank of America 3Q24 Earnings Press Release, October 15, 2024.
  5. Americans Are Falling Behind on Their Bills. Wall Street Is Alarmed., The Wall Street Journal, Angel Au-Yeung, September 14, 2024.
  6. Third Quarter 2024 Earnings Results, Goldman Sachs, October 15, 2024.
  7. Morgan Stanley Third Quarter 2024 Earnings Results, October 16, 2024.
  8. "JPMorgan Calls It: The U.S. Economy Has Made a Soft Landing," Wall Street Journal, Alexander Saeedy, October 11, 2024.
  9. ​Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, October 11, 2024.
  10. Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, October 11, 2024.
  11. Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, October 11, 2024.
  12. Earnings Insight, FactSet, John Butters, October 11, 2024.

Original Post

data.title: Madison Sustainable Equity Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Environment And Performance

The third quarter was strong across all equity classes. The S&P 500 was up 5.89% during the quarter, bringing the full year performance to 22.08%. Value outperformed growth during the quarter, with the Russell 1000 Value up 9.1% while the Russell 1000 Growth was up 3.2%. Small and mid cap stocks also performed quite well, up 9.3% (Russell 2000) and 9.2% (Russell Midcap). We also saw the market broaden out beyond the mega cap tech stocks during the third quarter.

With inflation readings continuing to shift lower and a job market that has softened a bit, the Federal Reserve cut interest rates in September by 50 basis points, which resulted in the yield curve shifting lower during the month. Although the economy continues to grow, there have been signs of slowing, with GDP expected to continue to moderate into year-end. In recent comments, the Fed has noted that they are turning their attention to unemployment rather than inflation as the main focus.

With inflation seemingly under control and some cracks in the job market, the market is expecting further rate cuts by the Fed into year-end.

More Specific Performance Attribution & Activity

Utilities, Real Estate, Industrials and Financials led the equity markets with double-digit returns in the quarter. Communications Services, Technology, and Energy were the weakest performing sectors. Communications Services and Technology were up 1.7% and 1.6%, respectively, while Energy was down -2.3% during the quarter. The Madison Sustainable Equity Fund (Class Y) outperformed the S&P 500 during the quarter. Sector Allocation was positive during the third quarter while Stock Selection was neutral. Sector Allocation was positive, with overweights to Utilities and Materials and underweights to Energy and Technology. Our underweights to Real Estate and Industrials detracted from performance. Stock selection was positive in Technology, Utilities, and Financials, but detracted in Communications Services, Consumer Staples, and Materials.

The top contributors in the quarter were NextEra Energy (NEE), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), Progressive Corporation (PGR), Equifax Inc. (EFX), and United Healthcare (OTCPK:UAHC). Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) were the largest detractors.

NextEra has continued to perform well given its strong position in the renewable energy space, increasing demand for power, its transmission capabilities, as well as a tailwind from lower interest rates. Oracle reported a strong first quarter with accelerating Cloud infrastructure demand. Oracle followed its solid first quarter with an upbeat analyst meeting which highlighted multi-year growth opportunities for the company. Progressive Corp continues to perform well in a strong personal auto market. August results were better than expected with strong margins and better than expected premium and Policy in Force growth.

Although Equifax had a slightly mixed quarter, we believe that investors became more interested in the stock with the expectation that interest rates would come down and an expected return to solid earnings growth for the company. The U.S. Mortgage market has been depressed, we anticipate that lower interest rates will have a positive impact on the housing market and, in turn, Equifax.

United Healthcare reported a solid second quarter with an earnings beat. The quarter was a bit messy, but the underlying results were strong. The quarter included costs related to the Change Healthcare outage; however, investors have shifted their focus to 2025 with expected Medicare Advantage growth and moderating headwinds from Medicaid Medical Loss Ratio.

Alphabet continued to report solid numbers in the second quarter with strong Search, Cloud, and better operating margins. However, attention has recently shifted to the potential remedies post the DOJ trial, where it found that Google’s agreements with mobile phone OEMs to pre-install Google Search were anti-competitive. This part of the ruling could lead to changes in how Google distributes its search engine. The market is uncertain about the extent of Google’s market share losses in paid search under various remedy scenarios, which has been a headwind for the stock.

After first half strength, Eli Lilly has traded in a range this quarter, despite dramatically raising revenues and earnings following their second quarter report. There is a lot of noise in the Diabetes-Obesity space as many companies are looking for opportunities to get into the market, which is expected to exceed $100 billion in revenues in 2030. We have not seen any competitor data that would dethrone Novo Nordisk or Lilly but are watching carefully. Manufacturing capacity is a key barrier to entry and Lilly and Novo have locked up capacity for the next several years. Qualcomm has given back some of its first half gains after the CFO commented at a conference that its entrance into the AI PC business would take time to ramp. We continue to see Qualcomm as well positioned with growth from AI moving into the mobile phone, from new opportunities on the Internet of Things (IoT), and within the Auto industry but will also look to future growth as they enter the PC market. Microsoft has also given back some performance after a strong first half move. Microsoft’s fiscal fourth quarter was slightly below expectations as Azure growth came in below expectations. Commentary remains quite bullish about the AI opportunity and Microsoft continues to point to supply constraints. We continue to see Microsoft as one of the better positioned companies to benefit from the proliferation of AI with their expansive enterprise position.

Apple has been volatile in the last quarter but ended on strength. Early in the quarter, Apple benefited from the introduction of their AI strategy, Apple Intelligence. They followed in September with the new iPhone 16, which also created some excitement. We are underweight to Apple, which has resulted in a headwind for performance.

We added Keysight Technologies (KEYS) and Charles Schwab (SCHW) to the portfolio. Keysight focuses on providing essential testing and measurement solutions across a broad spectrum of industries, including wireless, wireline, automotive, and aerospace & defense. The company has a long history of strong financial performance, characterized by consistent revenue growth and healthy operating margins. Charles Schwab has an attractive business model where the primary growth driver is growing assets from existing and new customers. Schwab was impacted by the interest rate hiking cycle by relying on high-cost funding sources as its customers moved their deposits to higher-yielding alternatives such as money market funds. As we are now heading towards lower interest rates, we expect Schwab to have lower interest expenses as high-cost funding decreases which should drive strong earnings-per-share growth over the next few years. To fund these new purchases, we sold Nestle as we believe that the company’s organic growth profile has deteriorated and that it may deliver lower profits as a result. The company also recently unexpectedly fired its CEO and replaced the executive with a long-term insider which may not lead to resolving its current challenges. We also sold Nike. Although Nike recently changed its CEO after several years of inconsistent fundamentals, we believe that a turnaround may take several years before investors see improved execution. Finally, we eliminated Pepsi, which was a modest position, as we had other more attractive investment opportunities.

Sustainable Notes

▶ Danaher (DHR) released its 2024 Corporate Sustainability Report. It highlighted several milestones across its three pillars of sustainability (building the best team, innovating products that improve lives and the planet, and protecting the environment). The company has committed to setting greenhouse gas emission reduction targets in line with the Science Based Targets initiatives (SBTi), including reaching net-zero value chain emissions by 2050.

▶ In early August, Microsoft held a call to discuss how to advance AI sustainably and responsibly with its Chief Sustainability Officer, its Chief Responsible AI Officer, and the CVP of Cloud Operations and Innovation. Each leader detailed the company’s progress in their respective departments, noting the company’s commitments to carbon negativity, water positivity, and zero waste by 2030, which were initially set in 2020. The methods to achieve these goals include sustainable design and construction for its data centers, transitioning to more carbon-free power sources, using AI to enable existing transmission lines to bring on new RE capacity faster, replenish more water than it consumes, and engaging its suppliers to encourage them to adopt more sustainable practices. The company is aligned with the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and its four key functions: maintain a system for governing AI risks, documenting the risks associated with AI, measuring the likelihood and impact of AI risks, and implementing safeguards to protect against AI risks.

▶ Costco (COST) continues to demonstrate its commitment to sustainability by lowering its emissions. For example, it has converted its Kirkland Signature laundry packs from plastic tubs to a pouch. This has reduced plastic packaging by 80%. It has also moved to localize production of bulky items such as water, paper, and laundry detergents. Manufacturing these goods closer to the countries in which they are sold reduces emissions associated with shipping.

▶ The key sustainability highlights of The Charles Schwab Corporation include a solid governance framework, which includes a CSR/Sustainability committee and well-diversified Board, where 5 of the 16 members are women. The company recently granted a $1,000 stock option to all employees to ensure everyone has a stake in the company. We rate Schwab as Average due to the highly regulated industry in which it operates.

▶ Keysight Technologies has one of the lowest Sustainalytics Risk Scores (rating is 6.20, which is in the Negligible ESG Risk Score range of 0-10) due to its strong management of social and environmental issues. We have rated Keysight as Average, which reflects an opportunity for the company to improve board gender diversity and more direct board oversight of environmental and social issues.

Our Outlook And Positioning

We continue to be somewhat cautious as we enter the final quarter of the year. Equities have remained strong despite a slowing economy, a softening job market, an escalation of geopolitical issues and a tight U.S. election race. On the positive side, inflation is moving in the right direction and the Fed has started to cut rates. The lower interest rates may provide a tailwind to equities, but it will depend on economic growth and company earnings.

Valuations have continued to move higher and are now at 21x 2025 calendar earnings. As we noted last quarter, the mega cap stocks have a valuation premium due to their robust growth, but if you look more broadly, there are still opportunities at attractive valuations. Earnings estimates have remained relatively stable, with a slight tick lower following second quarter earnings. The market is looking for 9% earnings growth in 2024 for the S&P 500 and 14.8% for 2025. With a slowing economy, a softer job market, the almost 15% growth for next year seems high to us. We are redoubling our efforts when it comes to stock selection, looking for high quality companies with strong long-term growth prospects that are trading at attractive valuations and will use volatility to add to companies in our portfolio that have become more attractively valued.

Sincerely,

Maya Bittar & Dave Geisler

Disclosures

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

data.title: EssilorLuxottica: Ray-Ban Meta Glasses Continue To Impress
data.description:
EssilorLuxottica (OTCPK:ESLOF) is a French company known worldwide for its lenses, frames, and sunglasses. Recently, Q3 2024 was released, and the market reacted positively with +3%.

Over the past year, the stock has performed very well (+37.53%), and more and more investors are getting interested in this company. In particular, its popularity has increased greatly after the partnership with Meta (META) regarding the production of smart glasses, leveraging the Ray-Ban brand.

The collaboration between these two companies, although atypical, has so far proved to be a brilliant choice, and we are probably only at the beginning. There is no full-scale market for smart glasses yet, but more and more people are showing curiosity.

In addition to the news about Ray-Ban Meta glasses, ESLOF demonstrated some resilience in revenues in the East, where many high-fashion companies are facing serious issues; LVMH's recent quarterly report is proof of this. The Chinese are struggling as a result of the real estate crisis, yet ESLOF continues to grow there as well.

Highlights Q3 2024

Before moving on to the news related to Ray-Ban Meta Glasses, let's take a look at the overall results of the latest quarterly report.

Revenues totaled €6.43 billion, up 2.30% from Q3 2023 or 4% at constant exchange rates. The fastest growing geographic area was EMEA, +5%, but Asia-Pacific surprised with +4.50%. As anticipated, China's economic slowdown is weighing negatively on all high fashion companies, but this does not seem to affect ESLOF. Its eyewear continues to be in demand and are proving to be resilient to any macroeconomic environment.

In particular, driving growth in China have been the new Essilor® Stellest™, lenses that can slow the progression of myopia in children; compared to last year, they have seen 40% growth and 2 million pairs of lenses have been sold so far.

The only geographical area that was underwhelming was Latin America, with a 5.60% decrease. In any case, it is clear that the problem here was fluctuating exchange rates, because if they had not varied, ESLOF would have recorded double-digit growth of 10.80%. Therefore, the companies' brands are still highly valued in Latin America.

By the way, if we were to look at this figure on a longer time-frame, over the last 9 months Latin America is still growing albeit slightly (+1%).

Overall, the earnings results for this quarter were positive, but I think the enthusiasm in the market comes mainly from the news concerns the collaboration with Meta:

We are pleased to enlarge the duration of the partnership that we have with Meta and it's a partnership that will be a journey that we'll do together around wearable. It's a partnership that will last quite a few years, I would say that will take us to the next decade. And it's a partnership that will also encompass, presumably, I would say will encompass other brands.

It is a partnership that has certain aspects of exclusivity that for obvious reasons we can't disclose. But I would say, if I have to judge by the results that we've seen so far with Ray-Ban Meta, the success the Ray-Ban Meta has had in many geographies, in every geography, I would say where it's commercialized and sold, I should say that we're very hopeful and confident that this is going to be a very successful journey together.

Stefano Grassi, conference call Q3 2024.

At first the collaboration between these two companies was met with skepticism; after all, there was never any talk of smart glasses. As of today, however, it represents a reality and ESLOF and Meta will probably collaborate for many more years together; the foresight of both management is what I like most about these two companies.

At the moment, these glasses are not yet widely used around the world, but they are increasing their popularity exponentially. There are some countries where these glasses have more functionality rather than others, which is why it is currently difficult to tell in which country they are performing better.

Management has not released numbers regarding the sales and profitability of the smart glasses, but some enthusiasm is leaking out, especially in the U.S. where artificial intelligence features have been integrated. These characteristics are not yet widespread in Europe but may be coming soon and this is an important driver of growth.

In any case, even with fewer features, the results are still outstanding. Suffice it to say that in 60% of Ray-Ban stores in EMEA these glasses were the best-selling product. This is by no means a given since they are priced around €300.

Meta claimed to have sold 700,000 units at a minimum price of $299, this means $209.30 million in revenue. We do not know the profitability of this business and the details of the commercial agreement between the two companies, but I have high expectations. After all, ESLOF's economies of scale in this area are its strength, and Meta is one of the best companies in the world when it comes to innovation.

Finally, one last interesting aspect concerns the completion of the acquisitions of the Supreme brand and 80% of Heidelberg Engineering. In the first case, the company shelled out $1.50 billion and aims to revitalize a brand that has lost its popularity in recent years: ASLOF is looking to diversify its revenues and enter other segments of the fashion world as well. It is already part of it, but only through eyewear, and the time has come to expand its offerings.

As for the acquisition of Heidelberg Engineering, the matter is more complex. This company produces the machines through which ophthalmologists are able to reveal eye diseases or more generally do a checkup. There has never been a lot of information about it, but ASLOF's choice to buy this company could hide much greater potential than the market is discounting.

These are just guesses, but ASLOF may have bought it to be even more entrenched within its market. To date, we only buy its eyewear after having an eye examination, but in the future that may no longer be the case if it has the machinery in place to do it on its own; the time to buy a pair of lenses could become much shorter. Just think if there were a machine in Ray-Ban stores that could do a complete check-up of your eyes in seconds, so that you could immediately order new lenses. This procedure could even be done online, and at that point getting the new glasses would be as easy as buying a T-shirt on Amazon.

Of course, since the eye examination is a medical procedure, countless conditions would have to be met before getting to that point, and it will not be easy. The potential is there and ASLOF is laying the roots for a new growth driver.

Conclusion

ASLOF has always been engaged in selling lenses, but the latest maneuvers suggest that it may become something more. Strategic investments in Supreme and Heidelberg Engineering may on the one hand diversify revenues and on the other create a market that does not yet exist: our habits for buying a pair of lenses may change in the future.

Overall, the quarterly report revealed resilience, especially in China thanks to new lenses that can slow the progression of myopia. All geographies showed growing revenues, and the new Ray-Ban Meta glasses are proving to be a success. Gradually new features related to artificial intelligence will be released, and this could give a big boost to revenues.

The partnership between Meta and Ray-Ban is set to last for a long time, and together they could pioneer a highly innovative market. Before long, it could be normal to take a picture with glasses or to see the world under the lens of advanced reality.

It is still too complicated to quantify the potential of this partnership, but the few data available are promising. In Ray-Ban stores these glasses are in high demand despite the high price, and 700,000 units have already been sold at a minimum of $299. What if you could call, text or make a video call from your glasses? How much would you be willing to pay?

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Madison Small Cap Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
After lagging the large cap indices for most of the year, small caps outperformed in the third quarter, driven mostly by multiple expansion, as earnings continue to be relatively depressed relative to large caps.

Performance And Positioning

The Madison Small Cap Fund (Class Y) returned 6.3% in the third quarter of 2024. Performance lagged the Russell 2000's return of 9.3% and Russell 2500’s return of 8.8%. Underperformance was driven by both allocation and selection. Our laggard sectors in the third quarter included: Industrials, driven by stock selection; Healthcare, also driven by stock selection; and Information Technology, primarily driven by allocation effect. Energy outperformed, driven by allocation, and Consumer Discretionary also outperformed, driven by stock selection.

We made very few changes to the fund’s positioning in the third quarter. Our only new investment to the fund was Workiva (WK), a cloud-based software provider of compliance and regulatory reporting solutions, whose platform is used by thousands of companies to publish SEC and other regulatory documents.

More than 75% of the Fortune 500 use at least one of the solutions. The Workiva platform offers customers collaboration, data integration, and process management controls, which result in greater visibility and insights into their financial and regulatory management systems and enable efficient regulatory filing and reporting. Workiva is considered a system of record for compliance and regulatory reporting, a relatively non-discretionary category.

The company has a new CEO focused on profitable growth by building a partner channel with the influential Big 5 consulting firms. The competitive moats are wide and deep, as Workiva continues to press where it sees its greatest competitive advantage: being the only provider selling a single platform for financial and non-financial reporting with assurance.

Furthermore, the company has a significant installed base of existing customers (~6,000) ripe for harvesting. Our base case is that the installed software is very “sticky” with low turnover rates and durables growth runways. The current stock price represents a material discount relative to our conservative intrinsic value assessment of $100 per share. We've opportunistically used the combination of new management resetting expectations, broadly weak sentiment for software companies, and recent market volatility to establish this new position.

From a sector perspective, our overweights are in Consumer, Industrials, and Information Technology, which has been consistent for a few years. Long-time investors will know that we don’t make sector bets, these happen to be the areas where we’ve found the highest quality business at discounts to intrinsic value. Although our Industrial stocks represent the biggest drag to our performance in the third quarter, we are very optimistic as we look ahead. Many of our Industrial stocks are tied to the housing sector, which has been depressed for a few years. The housing market has been in a structural undersupply for several years (see the chart from Realtor.com). Residential construction has been pressured by high costs and a tight mortgage market. However, as we potentially begin a central bank rate easing cycle, the combination of undersupply vs strong secular demand and lower rates may result in a significant residential construction cycle lasting several years.

Although we continue to be underweight Healthcare, stock selection year-to-date has been generally strong. Our largest overweight remains Information Technology, where we have outperformed the Russell 2000 Index by about 1,000 basis points year to date, almost all driven by stock selection. Many of these investments occurred during 2022, when the sector was extremely out of favor, and we identified attractively growing, profitable technology companies trading at large discounts to their intrinsic value.

While there is some macro uncertainty in the market, specifically around the strength and durability of the labor market, consumer spending remains resilient, buoyed by higher wages and a halo of wealth effect from the strong market and higher housing prices. Many of our investments tied to the construction industry await signs that the industry has bottomed, and project starts could accelerate in early 2025, driven by nascent interest rate easing cycle and a potentially soft economic landing. We are closely watching the Architectural Billing Index for signs of an inflection. Thus far, it remains in slightly contraction territory.

One Final Note

As we reflect on our five years with Madison Investments, we’re reminded of the strong alignment between our investment philosophy and that of our teammates on the broader Madison U.S. Equity Department. When we joined the firm in the third quarter of 2019, the shared commitment to bottom-up, research-driven, high-conviction investing and a focus on risk management was immediately clear. Embracing the Participate and Protect approach that Madison Investments is known for came naturally. Our core investment principles remain unchanged. We continue to enhance our collaboration with our U.S. Equity colleagues in an effort to deliver the best risk-adjusted investment returns possible.

As always, we appreciate the opportunity to serve our clients.

Sincerely,

Faraz Farzam | Aaron Garcia

Disclosures

“Madison” and/or “Madison Investments” is the unifying tradename of Madison Investment Holdings, Inc., Madison Asset Management, LLC (“MAM”), and Madison Investment Advisors, LLC (“MIA”). MAM and MIA are registered as investment advisers with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC. MFD Distributor, LLC is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a broker-dealer, and is a member firm of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority. The home office for each firm listed above is 550 Science Drive, Madison, WI 53711. Madison’s toll-free number is 800-767-0300.

Any performance data shown represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

Russell 2000®Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 11% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000® Index. The Russell 3000 Index measures the performance of the largest 3,000 U.S. companies representing approximately 98% of the investable U.S. equity market. The Russell 3000 Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive, unbiased and stable barometer of the broad market and is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are reflected. Russell Investment Group is the source and owner of the trademarks, service marks and copyrights related to the Russell Indexes. Russell® is a trademark of Russell Investment Group.

The Russell 2500 Index combines a portion of midcap stocks with small cap stocks – forming a “SMID” (small/mid) cap segment of stocks from the Russell 3000®.

The Architecture Billings Index tracks the monthly billings of architecture firms, offering an advance look at nonresidential construction spending trends for the coming nine to twelve months. The index is derived from a monthly survey of architecture firms across the U.S., and measures the changes in the number of design services provided to clients, reflecting the demand for architectural services.

Madison’s expectation is that investors in the strategy will participate near fully in market appreciation during bull markets and experience something less than full participation during bear markets compared with investors in portfolios holding more speculative and volatile securities. Therefore, the investment philosophy is intended to represent a conservative investment strategy. There is no assurance that Madison’s expectations regarding this investment strategy will be realized.

A basis point is one hundredth of a percent.

Non-deposit investment products are not federally insured, involve investment risk, may lose value and are not obligations of, or guaranteed by, any financial institution. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate.

data.title: CF Industries: Probably The Ideal Fertilizer Investment
data.description:
CF Industries (NYSE:CF) is the world's leader in ammonia production. Initially requested by a friend of mine, I went into this analysis blind but came out reasonably impressed. CF is not only a well-run business; I think it's trading at a good price for long-term returns. That's why it's earned a Buy rating from me today.

Business Model

The company doesn't have a particularly complicated business (unless you want burden yourself with the deeper, scientific aspects). Its main product is anhydrous ammonia, which most of us know as simply ammonia. It also produces and sells derivative chemicals. As such, this is a commodity business, with ammonia as the commodity.

As the principal resource for creating ammonia is natural gas, this means the ammonia cycle is subject to trends for natural gas as well.

With a mixed product composition, CF can adjust as demand for different end uses fluctuate on seasonal and cyclical bases, using the same facilities.

Their assets chiefly consist of 8 production sites and 23 distribution centers located across North America.

Their facility in Donaldsonville, LA accounts for the bulk of all production and is the largest nitrogen plant in the world, a testament to the company's leading role in ammonia production.

These chemical products are mainly used to develop fertilizers, so their customers largely consist of farmer's cooperatives and others down the supply chain. Products also serve certain industrial uses not necessarily related to agriculture.

Financial History

Over the past decade, CF's business model has allowed for steady improvement for shareholders.

Revenue was flat for much of the decade, but after COVID hit and Russia invaded Ukraine (the latter being more significant), CF's operating structure left it well positioned as the top and bottom lines are concerned. According to their disclosures, increases in the cost of natural gas for European competitors caused them to reduce production, as their margins were strained.

Natural gas isn't just an energy source; it's a chemical used to create ammonia. As European companies seek alternatives to Russian natural gas, that's easier to do for energy but not ammonia. Additionally, the invasion disrupted Black Sea trade routes, with Ukraine being a major exporter of global grain. As this drove food prices up, many of CF's customers were willing to pay higher rates for ammonia to satisfy this under-supply.

Seen above, price hikes allowed by these parallel situations increased what I've nicknamed "common shares' free cash flow," or CFCF (a figure I will explain later).

It's also worth explaining why the company's cash flows were negative at the start of the decade and went positive, especially when revenues weren't increasing.

The primary reason was a sharp decline in capex that occurred between 2016 and 2017, as capital projects for most of the current facilities were completed. Since then, capex levels have remained very stable. CF rarely makes acquisitions but did make one in 2023 for $1.2B (the Waggamon, LA facility).

What I find interesting here is that the acquisition was made without incurring any additional debt, meaning that the company felt comfortable financing the acquisition entirely with its own cash. This is also on a backdrop of successfully reducing total debt once the capital improvements were made.

This remaining $3B in debt is mostly due a decade out or more, and the interest around 5% is fairly inexpensive. Following the debt reduction in recent years, CF usually has a couple of billion in cash on hand. This points to a very healthy balance sheet.

The preceding phases of the last decade explain the evolution of returns to shareholders. Once capex declined and the total debt was reduced, dividend distributions could be more readily increased, and buybacks stepped up as well.

Future Outlook

Overall, I think CF is in one of the best positions it's ever had and that the future for shareholders ahead is a positive one. I will discuss some of the risks that come with it as a commodity and cyclical play, but I remain confident that it will be accretive over the long run.

Steady Capacity

Capex has remained flat compared to older levels and was not stepped up in response to the under-supply of ammonia over the last three years. As the leader in ammonia production, CF was content with its current capacity and ability to raise prices.

Moreover, management is guiding for very light growth of nitrogen demand over time, clearly not pressured to burn cash on capex. As such, I think it would take some kind of paradigm shift, a disturbance in the current order, to force them to step it up.

Continued Buybacks and Dividends

As free cash flow remains positive, I expect the buybacks and the dividends to continue.

Comfortable with the current operation and with approval for about $1.9B in repurchases, management has been very clear that they intend to use it. The main risk here is, perhaps, that the price could increase rapidly, leading to poor pricing on the buybacks. This guidance also suggests that a hefty dividend increase will not occur until after 2025, depending on what the board approves for future repurchases.

As the history is one that seems to favor buybacks, I expect that to continue, and part of the return will be in how FCF per share is grown through this process.

Valuation

To value shares of CF, I'm using a Discounted Cash Flow model, using the CFCF figure I mentioned before. The figure is essentially free cash flow, after deducting the earnings attributable to noncontrolling interests (some of their assets have minority ownership by a second party). This attempts to take FCF from the consolidated results and avoid counting earnings not owned by the common.

With that in mind, I'll do the calculation with the following assumptions:

  • $9.15 as baseline CFCF per share
  • 4% CAGR the next decade
  • Terminal multiple of 8

Averaging CFCF over the last five years to capture the highs and lows of that period and dividing it by Q2's shares outstanding gives us $9.45 per share. As 2023's M&A shows they will pay with their own cash, I've trimmed the figure a bit to factor in the occasional acquisition and make the estimate a bit more conservative.

As global nitrogen demand is expected to grow 1.5% going forward, and as the buybacks will also grow FCF per share, I think it's reasonable to assume that 4% growth can be achieved. I also think it's unlikely that such a well-capitalized leader in ammonia production would trade for a low multiple, but 8 is what we get right now with today's market price, based on my CFCF figure, and I see nothing wrong with pricing for that going forward as well.

Priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of the market), that makes a fair value almost $110 per share, a 22% discount! Given the modest margin of safety for such a quality company, I think this makes for an exceptionally attractive investment.

Risks

As this is priced for a return over a ten-year period of time, there are plenty of opportunities for something to challenge my valuation. With their European competitors being a model for how this type of business can suffer, I think the "paradigm shift" I mentioned before would largely be political in nature, just as the situation with Russia was.

Here in America, however, I expect it would have more to do with environmental concerns. I think anyone who's used ammonia-based cleaners before can generally imagine why there might be environmental concerns, but I want to highlight something that changed in the company's tone.

The screenshots above show the opening passages to the 10Ks of 2020 and 2021. 2020's, when Trump was still President, speaks more dryly to what CF is. 2021's, published right as Biden has taken office, stresses clean energy and emission abatement. This says to me that the company is sensitive to political perceptions.

I've noticed that when we call it an energy source, it's natural gas, but when discussing its impact to climate change, it's methane. Yes, methane is a greenhouse gas, and natural gas wells are infamous for leakages that release more into the atmosphere. Attempts by the government to combat this by restricting natural gas here in the US could put CF in a similar boat as their European peers with tighter margins.

To a lesser extent, there is rising sentiment (politically and culturally) for organically grown foods that do not use artificial fertilizers. Thus, supply of an essential material is one risk, and declining demand for ammonia-driven farming is another. If either of these materialize to a significant degree in the next decade, my valuation may not be so discounted.

Q3 Outlook

As Q3 results approach, we will want to see how many buybacks occurred, which may inform an improved CFCF per share figure.

As they report more activity in the harvest season, we'll also want to observe if there's any irregularities in this year's weather patterns and that may upset customer depend, as this could result in a near-term blow to revenues.

Conclusion

CF Industries is a good company that had a much rougher start to the previous decade. Yet, its capital projects bore fruit. This allowed deleveraging and demonstrated its advantages when disruptions in Europe weakened their peers over there. With low debt, steady buybacks, and dividends, I expect CF to do quite well.

The risks aren't zero, but I do believe they are easy to imagine and perhaps see coming. If folks can brace for any regulatory hurdles, the $86 share price we see today seems attractively undervalued for a long-term position, and that's why I find it be one of my more confident Buy ratings.
